title: The Mook The Chef The Wife And Her Homer

jokes.text: Otto, Bart won't give me a seat. / You know I can deal with your problem or I can rock out. But I cannot do both.
jokes.setup: Child asks adult authority figure for help with basic problem
jokes.punchline: Bus driver prioritizes rocking out over child safety
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto characterization - irresponsible adult who treats music as more important than children's welfare. Clean setup/punchline structure with great economy.

jokes.text: Fu-u-u-u... nk.
jokes.setup: Otto starts to swear in front of children
jokes.punchline: Censors himself mid-word with stretched-out fake word
jokes.explanation: Standard censorship gag. Works for character but not particularly original or memorable.

jokes.text: What am I hearing now? Nature? The ultimate bring-down. Why... was I... born?!
jokes.setup: Radio stops working, leaving Otto in silence
jokes.punchline: Otto has existential crisis over hearing nature sounds
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Otto's dependence on music is so complete that nature sounds trigger existential dread. Absurdist but grounded in character.

jokes.text: Old McDonald had a fart Fart, fart, fart, fart, fart
jokes.setup: Kids offer to help Otto with 'tunes'
jokes.punchline: Bart's idea of music is juvenile fart version of classic song
jokes.explanation: Generic juvenile humor. Expected from Bart but lacks wit or surprise.

jokes.text: That's not a song. Real songs are about deals with the devil, far-off lands, and where you'd find smoke in relation to water.
jokes.setup: Otto dismisses Bart's fart song
jokes.punchline: Otto's pretentious definition of 'real songs' references classic rock tropes
jokes.explanation: Great Otto moment - references 'Crossroads,' 'Kashmir,' and 'Smoke on the Water' while maintaining his stoner philosopher persona.

jokes.text: One of my bus mates has purloined my French horn.
jokes.setup: Martin needs to report theft
jokes.punchline: Martin uses overly formal language ('purloined') for simple playground theft
jokes.explanation: Classic Martin - unnecessarily verbose vocabulary for mundane situation. Character-appropriate but predictable.

jokes.text: Why can't you talk like a dude?
jokes.setup: Martin speaks formally
jokes.punchline: Kearney, a repeat third-grader, criticizes Martin's vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Good economy and character consistency. Kearney as anti-intellectual bully is well-established.

jokes.text: Oh, knock it off, Kearney. Why are you still in this school anyway? We were in third grade together.
jokes.setup: Bart defends Martin
jokes.punchline: Reveals Kearney has been held back for years - he and Bart were classmates
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about educational failure. Good reveal that explains Kearney's age/size discrepancy with other students.

jokes.text: Stupid school doesn't know how to teach me.
jokes.setup: Kearney is revealed to be held back multiple grades
jokes.punchline: He blames the school instead of taking responsibility
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to previous joke. Kearney's lack of self-awareness is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Oh, wow, this is about 90% less funny than you think it is.
jokes.setup: Kids singing annoying fart songs on bus
jokes.punchline: Otto gives precise statistical assessment of their humor's failure
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The specificity of '90%' and Otto's deadpan delivery make this highly quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Am I on drugs? / Yes, you are, but that really is Metallica.
jokes.setup: Otto questions if he's hallucinating seeing Metallica
jokes.punchline: He confirms he is on drugs but Metallica is still real
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto moment. Self-aware about drug use while maintaining wonder at celebrity encounter. Great structure and economy.

jokes.text: Now if you'll excuse me, I'm late for a gumdrop parade on Foo-foo Island.
jokes.setup: Otto acknowledges he's on drugs
jokes.punchline: Describes whimsical hallucination with childlike imagery
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to drug admission. The innocent imagery contrasts nicely with drug use theme.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, Springfield Arena, 1997, Row XX, Seat 64.
jokes.setup: Otto tries to prove he's a real Metallica fan
jokes.punchline: Remembers exact seat location from concert years ago
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Otto's music obsession. Realistic fan behavior.

jokes.text: Look at me, I'm Otto. I'm a hundred years old, and I drive a school bus.
jokes.setup: Metallica needs transportation
jokes.punchline: They mock Otto's life choices to his face
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe humor. Shows Otto's heroes rejecting him for his mundane life.

jokes.text: I used to sleep with Lars' grandmother.
jokes.setup: Competition for who's the bigger Metallica fan
jokes.punchline: Extremely inappropriate and unlikely claim about band member's grandmother
jokes.explanation: Shocking and absurd claim. High economy but lacks character grounding since it's a random driver.

jokes.text: Never listen to our music again.
jokes.setup: Otto loses Metallica to another fan
jokes.punchline: They ban him from listening to their music
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Otto's rejection. Absurd authority claimed by musicians over their fans.

jokes.text: Bye-bye, take care, watch your step, eat my shorts, have a good one.
jokes.setup: Otto saying goodbye to school bus
jokes.punchline: Mixes polite farewells with Bart's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Mild callback humor. Otto's confused emotional state shown through mixing registers.

jokes.text: Oh, and I think I ran over a moose.
jokes.setup: Otto hands over bus duties
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions possibly killing large animal
jokes.explanation: Great Otto moment - casual delivery of potentially serious information. Perfect character irresponsibility.

jokes.text: Can't... talk... now. I'm spanking a child.
jokes.setup: Principal asks if Otto is administering corporal punishment
jokes.punchline: Otto's response sounds inappropriate out of context
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke. Otto's phrasing makes legitimate discipline sound wrong.

jokes.text: And your gun.
jokes.setup: Otto suspended, must return bus items
jokes.punchline: Reveals bus driver was armed
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation and timing. Unexpected reveal that school bus driver carries weapon. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Let me drive. I go through yellow lights.
jokes.setup: Marge has to drive carpool
jokes.punchline: Bart offers to drive based on minor traffic violation experience
jokes.explanation: Good Bart logic - thinks minor rule-breaking qualifies him for major responsibility.

jokes.text: One more crack out of you, young man, and I'm showing everyone your baby pictures.
jokes.setup: Marge needs to discipline Bart in front of his friends
jokes.punchline: Threatens embarrassment instead of traditional punishment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge parenting - understanding what would actually mortify Bart. Character-appropriate threat.

jokes.text: I brought my own car seat. Look.
jokes.setup: Kids getting into carpool
jokes.punchline: Ralph brings car seat despite being school-aged
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph behavior - childlike actions beyond appropriate age. Character-consistent.

jokes.text: Hey, Lisa, my safety bar matches your eyes.
jokes.setup: Ralph trying to be friendly in carpool
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate romantic comment comparing car equipment to Lisa's features
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph - inappropriate romantic gesture with innocent delivery. Good cringe factor.

jokes.text: Whoa. 'Squirrels don't like rocks.'
jokes.setup: Nelson finishing his science project
jokes.punchline: Project conclusion is obvious and unscientific
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor. The obviousness and anti-scientific nature make it funny. Good economy and quotability.

jokes.text: Ew, it's Michael. That weird kid who never says anything.
jokes.setup: Kids react to seeing Michael
jokes.punchline: They call him weird for not talking, then all greet him
jokes.explanation: Setup for character introduction but not particularly funny on its own. Lisa being judgmental is out of character.

jokes.text: Hey, dork, you're sittin' on my shadow. / Sorry. / What, you're too good to sit on my shadow?
jokes.setup: Kearney bullying Michael about sitting position
jokes.punchline: No matter what Michael does, Kearney finds fault with imaginary shadow offense
jokes.explanation: Excellent bully logic - creates no-win situation with absurd complaint about shadows. Perfect Kearney characterization and structure.

jokes.text: Whoa. Your mother must dance at the nicest strip club in town.
jokes.setup: Homer sees Michael's impressive house
jokes.punchline: Assumes wealth comes from mother's sex work rather than legitimate business
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - inappropriate assumption delivered as compliment. Good misdirection and economy.

jokes.text: Here is your book, and never forget: the divisor goes into the dividend.
jokes.setup: Intimidating mob boss bringing child's school book
jokes.punchline: Gives fatherly math advice in menacing voice
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - mob boss as caring father. Juxtaposition of menace and parental concern works perfectly.

jokes.text: There is no Mafia. Columbus Day is better than Christmas.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony responds to Homer's panic
jokes.punchline: Combines mob denial with random Italian-American pride statement
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor. The random Columbus Day statement after Mafia denial is perfectly surreal and quotable.

jokes.text: My daddy had to potty in a bag.
jokes.setup: Ralph continues explaining his father's shooting
jokes.punchline: Uses childish vocabulary ('potty') for serious medical consequence
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to previous joke. Ralph's innocent vocabulary makes dark subject matter absurd.

jokes.text: Ah, look at all this puke! Why did I come in on my day off?
jokes.setup: Willie encounters mess at school
jokes.punchline: Questions his own dedication while complaining
jokes.explanation: Typical Willie - gruff complaint about working conditions. Character-appropriate but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I... from now on you're not third clarinet, you're first everything!
jokes.setup: Teacher trying to make up for almost insulting Lisa
jokes.punchline: Overcompensates with impossible promotion to 'first everything'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of teacher's panic. 'First everything' is absurd but shows desperation to appease.

jokes.text: Now if you excuse me, I'm brewing tea and I have to go jiggle my bags!
jokes.setup: Teacher making excuse to leave awkward situation
jokes.punchline: Innocent tea preparation sounds inappropriate out of context
jokes.explanation: Standard double entendre humor. Functional but not particularly clever or character-specific.

jokes.text: There's a triple-A battery in my macaroni and cheese!
jokes.setup: Michael examining school lunch
jokes.punchline: Finds electronic component in food
jokes.explanation: Standard school lunch quality joke. Absurd but expected for Simpsons school food.

jokes.text: It counts as a vegetable.
jokes.setup: Michael complains about battery in food
jokes.punchline: Lunch lady categorizes battery as vegetable
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery. Perfect Lunch Lady Doris - dismissive and absurd. Great economy.

jokes.text: It's delicious! / Are you just saying that 'cause you're afraid of my dad? / No, it's great! Ooh! Except for the bee!
jokes.setup: Lisa tastes Michael's cooking and compliments it
jokes.punchline: Bee sting interrupts genuine praise
jokes.explanation: Good timing with bee interruption. Shows Lisa's honesty despite potential danger from mob connection.

jokes.text: We are going for a ride. By which I mean the carpool. Perhaps we will get yogurt.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony uses ominous mob phrase 'going for a ride'
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he means literal carpool and innocent yogurt trip
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. Perfect Fat Tony - unconsciously uses threatening language for innocent activities. Great structure.

jokes.text: Now who wants to sleep with the fishes? Because I brought this Finding Nemo bedspread.
jokes.setup: Another ominous mob phrase about sleeping with fishes
jokes.punchline: Reveals literal fish-themed bedspread for kids
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to previous joke structure. Finding Nemo reference makes it contemporary and wholesome. Excellent misdirection and character work.

jokes.text: 'Garfield' or 'Love Is?' / Uh, I prefer the cat. He hates Mondays. We can all relate.
jokes.setup: Milhouse offers binder choices to mob boss
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony makes relatable observation about Garfield's Monday hatred
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Fat Tony's humanity through pop culture observation. Universal Monday experience.

jokes.text: Sadly, my Anna Maria was whacked by natural causes.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about Fat Tony's wife
jokes.punchline: Uses mob terminology ('whacked') for natural death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay. Perfect Fat Tony - can't escape mob vocabulary even for personal tragedy. Excellent economy and memorability.

jokes.text: Ooh, flowers every week! I wish I was dead.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony mentions bringing flowers to his wife's grave weekly
jokes.punchline: Marge expresses envy of dead woman's treatment
jokes.explanation: Great Marge moment - domestic frustration leads to dark humor. Perfect economy and relatable marital complaint.

jokes.text: Actually, you can really keep costs down when you don't pay for materials or labor... or permits... or land.
jokes.setup: Homer comments on expensive-looking house
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony lists increasingly illegal cost-cutting methods
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure. Each item reveals deeper criminality. Perfect Fat Tony casual admission of crimes.

jokes.text: Your paintings have brush marks. And your statues have wieners!
jokes.setup: Homer admires Fat Tony's art collection
jokes.punchline: Homer appreciates art for wrong reasons - technical flaws and nudity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - misunderstands high art completely. Childish appreciation of nudity and technical ignorance.

jokes.text: He's talkin' like the guy in Fat Albert. / Howba areba youba? / Homer! / Whyba youba doba thatba?
jokes.setup: Fat Tony speaks Italian
jokes.punchline: Homer mocks it by referencing cartoon character, continues despite Marge's horror
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - cultural insensitivity despite danger. Marge's reaction adds to cringe factor.

jokes.text: I thought you meant 'hot-sync' it. You know how it is with us-- everything means kill.
jokes.setup: Legs explains why he destroyed the Palm Pilot
jokes.punchline: Admits mob tendency to interpret everything as murder instruction
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware humor about mob tropes. 'Hot-sync' misunderstanding is perfect tech/violence wordplay.

jokes.text: We meant no disrespect, Fat Tony. We were simply trying to kill you.
jokes.setup: Rival mob family explains their recent actions
jokes.punchline: Treats attempted murder as minor misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd logic. Treats assassination attempt as polite business disagreement. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: I made soufflés!
jokes.setup: Tense mob negotiation about Michael's potential for vengeance
jokes.punchline: Michael interrupts with innocent cooking announcement
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and juxtaposition. Michael's innocent interruption of death threats is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: I've tried to make those, but they always end up as brownies.
jokes.setup: Rival mobster responds to soufflé announcement
jokes.punchline: Shares domestic cooking failure during mob meeting
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to soufflé interruption. Shows mobsters have domestic sides too.

jokes.text: My God, this is like a lap dance for my taste buds!
jokes.setup: Fat Tony tastes Michael's dessert
jokes.punchline: Uses inappropriate sexual metaphor for food experience
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate crude metaphor. Fat Tony's lack of filter in front of son creates discomfort.

jokes.text: The flavor just drove my sweet tooth to a vacant lot and whacked it.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony continues praising dessert
jokes.punchline: Uses mob murder terminology for intense flavor experience
jokes.explanation: Excellent Fat Tony moment - cannot escape mob vocabulary even for positive experiences. Creative and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Hey, look what we got here. It's Chef Boy-ar-gay!
jokes.setup: Michael announces he wants to be a chef
jokes.punchline: Mobster makes homophobic pun on Chef Boyardee brand
jokes.explanation: Predictable and offensive pun. Character-appropriate for crude mobster but lacks wit or originality.

jokes.text: Well, gazpacho is served cold. So take that.
jokes.setup: Mobster threatens to serve soup cold
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects him with actual culinary knowledge
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's culinary knowledge defeating mob threat. Smart and defiant.

jokes.text: Do they small down big ones or do they make 'em different?
jokes.setup: Calabresi mentions liking cocktail hot dogs
jokes.punchline: Genuine curiosity about manufacturing process mid-threat
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing mobster's genuine curiosity about mundane topics during intimidation.

jokes.text: With Fat Tony doing the morphine mambo, the Calabresis are gonna try to put him down for a dirt nap.
jokes.setup: Explaining Fat Tony's medical situation
jokes.punchline: Uses colorful euphemisms for drug-induced unconsciousness and murder
jokes.explanation: 'Morphine mambo' is particularly creative. Perfect mob euphemistic language for serious medical and criminal situations.

jokes.text: Homer, our house is on fire! Help me save the children! / Sorry, Marge, can't hear you!
jokes.setup: Marge urgently calls for help with house fire
jokes.punchline: Homer ignores family emergency to continue mob fantasy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - ignores family crisis for less important activities. Good escalation of his irresponsibility.

jokes.text: You are a hybrid: half idiot, half moron.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about test driving hybrid car
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony creates insult using hybrid concept
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay using Homer's car request. Good structure and economy. Character-appropriate insult style.

jokes.text: Everything! And I learned it all from the greatest gangster film ever: Shark Tale.
jokes.setup: Homer claims to know about being mob boss
jokes.punchline: Cites children's animated fish movie as gangster education
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - completely wrong reference for gangster knowledge. Shark Tale vs. actual crime films is absurd.

jokes.text: I hurt my fist and my palm.
jokes.setup: Homer attempts to intimidate debtor
jokes.punchline: Injures himself in failed tough-guy act
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy failure. Character-appropriate for Homer but predictable slapstick.

jokes.text: Could I just pay you $5 to keep out Hardee's?
jokes.setup: Krusty can't afford full protection payment
jokes.punchline: Tries to negotiate cheaper rate for excluding lesser competitor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty - always looking for cheaper deal even under threat. Good business logic applied to extortion.

jokes.text: Ow! My schnoz! My punim! My pupik! My genechtagazoink!
jokes.setup: Krusty getting beaten up for non-payment
jokes.punchline: Uses increasingly ridiculous Yiddish terms for body parts
jokes.explanation: Excellent Krusty moment. Escalating absurdity of Yiddish terms, with final made-up word being highlight.

jokes.text: Uh, it-it fell off a truck. Uh, you know, a truck-truck.
jokes.setup: Marge asks where Homer got truck
jokes.punchline: Homer explains with classic mob excuse, then doubles down awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Good Homer attempt at mob speak. 'Truck-truck' shows his confusion about his own lie.

jokes.text: Eh, it fell off a truck-truck truck.
jokes.setup: Follow-up to previous truck explanation
jokes.punchline: Continues absurd escalation of truck-falling-off-truck concept
jokes.explanation: Good callback escalation. Homer's logic breaks down further with infinite truck regression.

jokes.text: Hi-diddily-ho, mob-areenos!
jokes.setup: Ned encounters Homer's mob operation
jokes.punchline: Combines his signature greeting style with mob terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders moment. Cannot abandon cheerful speech patterns even when addressing criminals. Highly quotable and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Dad, you want I should plug him in the ankle?
jokes.setup: Bart offering to hurt Ned
jokes.punchline: Uses mob speak but suggests non-lethal ankle shooting
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - tries to be tough but suggests least threatening violence possible. Child-appropriate mob threat.

jokes.text: You monster! Just cut his Achilles tendon with this knife.
jokes.setup: Homer responds to Bart's ankle shooting suggestion
jokes.punchline: Calls Bart monster then suggests more precise torture method
jokes.explanation: Good escalation. Homer's hypocritical moral outrage while suggesting worse violence is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Yeah. You know, they've still got to eat right and exercise. Walk the dog once in a while. He's a furry little fitness machine.
jokes.setup: Mobsters can only guarantee not to hurt Simpson family
jokes.punchline: Gives health and pet care advice to murder targets
jokes.explanation: Good juxtaposition of menace and genuine health concern. Unexpected caring advice from threatening mobster.

jokes.text: Thanks, but I'm going into bootleg DVDs.
jokes.setup: Calabresi offers Bart future in crime family
jokes.punchline: Bart chooses different but still illegal career path
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - rejects traditional crime for modern alternative. Shows entrepreneurial criminal spirit.

jokes.text: Hey, if I poisoned them, would I be doing this?
jokes.setup: Accused of poisoning meatballs
jokes.punchline: Homer demonstrates innocence by eating suspected poison
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - proves innocence through potentially dangerous action. Character-appropriate foolishness.

jokes.text: Don't ever ask me out my business, Lisa.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks if poisoning was accidental
jokes.punchline: Michael uses classic Godfather line, revealing his dark side
jokes.explanation: Excellent character reveal. Shows Michael may be more like his father than appeared. Perfect callback to classic film line.

tmdb_overview: Lisa's friendship with Fat Tony's son Michael prompts mob boss Tony to invite the Simpsons over for dinner. Michael says he'd rather be a chef than take over the family business, but that's before Tony is shot by a rival mobster.
